subject: English
title: Comprehension Passage 1
passage_text: For many young Nigerians, the city of Lagos represents both opportunity and struggle. Migrants arrive daily from smaller towns, drawn by the promise of jobs in commerce, technology, and entertainment. Yet the same city that offers a chance at prosperity also tests newcomers with overcrowded transport, high cost of housing, and fierce competition for work. Sociologists studying urban migration note that this tension between opportunity and hardship is not unique to Lagos; it mirrors patterns seen in fast-growing cities across the developing world. What distinguishes Lagos, however, is the sheer scale and speed of its growth, which has outpaced the infrastructure meant to support it.

subject: Geography
title: Population Data — West African City
passage_text: The table below shows the estimated population of a West African coastal city over four census periods: 1991 - 5.7 million; 2001 - 9.0 million; 2011 - 13.4 million; 2021 - 15.9 million. Urban planners attribute the acceleration between 1991 and 2011 primarily to rural-urban migration, while the slower growth after 2011 is linked to the rise of satellite towns absorbing new residents just outside the city's formal boundaries.
